title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Childhood Emotional Neglect by Extended Family
excerpt: Discover how somatic therapy helps survivors of childhood emotional neglect by extended family heal their nervous system and reclaim their embodied self.
body:
Understanding Childhood Emotional Neglect by Extended Family

When we think about childhood emotional neglect (CEN), the focus often lands on parents or primary caregivers. Yet, extended family members—grandparents, aunts, uncles, or older cousins—can also play a significant role in a child's emotional landscape. Emotional neglect isn’t always about abuse or overt mistreatment; it’s the absence of emotional attunement, responsiveness, and validation over time. As Bessel van der Kolk reminds us, trauma isn’t just about the event but also the relational context in which it happens. When extended family members emotionally neglect a child, that child’s nervous system learns to survive without essential emotional nourishment outside the immediate family unit, often intensifying feelings of invisibility and confusion.

What Does Emotional Neglect by Extended Family Look Like?

  • Ignoring the child’s emotional needs during family gatherings.
  • Dismissal or minimization of the child's feelings or experiences.
  • Lack of physical affection or comforting touch from relatives.
  • Overlooking the child’s achievements or expressions of distress.
  • Implicit messages that the child is unworthy of attention beyond the nuclear family.

Unlike direct abuse, this neglect is often invisible and can be difficult to articulate, which is why many survivors feel stuck even after years of therapy.

Why Somatic Therapy is Essential for Healing from CEN

Traditional talk therapy can offer insight, but it often falls short in addressing the deep, embodied impact of emotional neglect. Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, teaches that trauma is stored in the body, not just the mind. The nervous system remembers what the conscious mind forgets or represses. Children who experienced emotional neglect from extended family often carry a chronic state of under-arousal or hypervigilance in their nervous systems.

Somatic therapy focuses on reconnecting survivors to their bodily sensations and nervous system regulation. This approach helps survivors:

  • Recognize and validate their internal experiences.
  • Release stored tension and dysregulation.
  • Develop new, healthier nervous system responses.
  • Rebuild trust in their own bodily signals and emotional needs.

The Nervous System Impact of Extended Family Neglect

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a roadmap to understand the physiological imprint of neglect. When a child’s emotional needs are consistently unmet by extended family, their autonomic nervous system may default to shutdown (dorsal vagal state) or hyperarousal (sympathetic state) during family interactions. These responses are survival mechanisms, not flaws.

In other words, feeling numb or overly anxious around extended family members is a nervous system adaptation, not a personal failing. Recognizing this can empower survivors to approach healing with compassion rather than self-judgment.

“The body keeps the score, and somatic therapy helps rewrite that score by restoring safety and connection within the nervous system.” — Bessel van der Kolk

Practical Somatic Techniques to Begin Self-Healing

Survivors can begin somatic healing independently, even before working with a therapist trained in trauma. Here are foundational techniques supported by somatic researchers:

  • Body Scan Awareness: Slow, mindful attention to different body parts to notice tension or numbness (Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy).
  • Resourcing: Identifying internal or external sensations, places, or memories that evoke safety, as Deb Dana suggests in her work on Polyvagal Theory.
  • Grounding Exercises: Techniques like feeling the feet on the floor or placing hands on the belly to reorient to the present moment and stabilize the nervous system.
  • Movement and Tremoring: Gentle shaking or stretching to discharge autonomic arousal, a core principle in Levine’s Somatic Experiencing.
  • Breath Regulation: Slow, deep breathing to engage the parasympathetic nervous system and reduce hypervigilance.

Working with a Somatic Therapist: What to Expect

When survivors take the step to work with a somatic therapist, the process often begins by establishing safety and trust, crucial for those who have learned not to expect emotional attunement from extended family.

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy integrates talk therapy with body awareness, helping clients notice how trauma shows up physically. Therapists may guide survivors to track sensations, experiment with small movements, or engage in visualization to gently access stored emotions.

Therapists trained in Francine Shapiro’s EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing) may combine somatic techniques to process traumatic memories linked to neglect. Gabor Maté emphasizes the importance of compassion and understanding the developmental context of trauma, which aligns with the attunement-focused approach of somatic therapy.

Rebuilding Internal Attachment and Self-Compassion

Extended family neglect can leave survivors with fragile internal attachment models—meaning their sense of being worthy and lovable is deeply compromised. Dan Siegel’s work on interpersonal neurobiology highlights how attuned relationships shape the brain and nervous system. Somatic therapy helps survivors create new internal experiences of safety and connection.

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems model complements somatic work by helping survivors identify and soothe protective parts of themselves that developed in response to neglect. This internal re-parenting is a powerful step to reclaiming the emotional nourishment missed in childhood.

Moving Forward: Integrating Somatic Healing into Daily Life

Healing from childhood emotional neglect by extended family is not linear. It requires patience, consistent practice, and self-compassion. Survivors can integrate somatic tools into their daily routine by:

  • Checking in with bodily sensations multiple times a day.
  • Using grounding techniques before and after family interactions.
  • Creating physical rituals of safety, like wrapping in a weighted blanket or holding a comforting object.
  • Journaling sensations and emotions alongside traditional therapy work.
  • Building supportive community relationships that provide the emotional attunement once missing.

Ultimately, somatic therapy offers a path to not just survive but to thrive, rewiring the nervous system to experience safety, connection, and emotional vitality.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Penguin.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. Norton.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No: The Cost of Hidden Stress. Wiley.
  • Siegel, D. (2012). The Developing Mind: How Relationships and the Brain Interact to Shape Who We Are. Guilford Press.
  • Schwartz, R. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Shapiro, F. (2017). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: What is childhood emotional neglect by extended family?
faqs.a: It’s when extended family members consistently fail to meet a child’s emotional needs, leading to feelings of invisibility and abandonment beyond the nuclear family.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy help survivors of emotional neglect?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy reconnects survivors to their bodily sensations, helping regulate the nervous system and release stored trauma caused by neglect.

faqs.q: Can I do somatic healing work on my own?
faqs.a: Yes, foundational techniques like body scans, grounding, and breathwork can be practiced independently to begin calming the nervous system.

faqs.q: When should I seek a somatic therapist?
faqs.a: If emotional neglect impacts your daily life, relationships, or sense of safety, working with a trained somatic therapist can provide personalized support and healing.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: This seminal book explains how trauma is stored in the body and offers insights into somatic healing for trauma survivors.

metaTitle: Somatic Therapy for Childhood Emotional Neglect Survivors
metaDescription: Learn how somatic therapy supports survivors of childhood emotional neglect by extended family to heal nervous system trauma and reclaim emotional health.
author: The Oracle Lover
